Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd study when I felt like it, which wasn't that often. Some weeks I'd put in maybe 15 hours, but it was scattered and unfocused. I'd usually just read through materials without a real plan, maybe highlight some stuff. The sadness I've been dealing with made it hard to stick to any kind of schedule.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything, then cram when something was due. Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. Regular times? That sounds nice, but I just couldn't make myself care enough to set that up. Everything felt like too much effort most days.
Q3: Usually a deadline reminder or seeing a notification on my phone. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if there was anything urgent I was missing. I wasn't excited to be there or anything, it was more like checking to make sure I wasn't completely failing. The CBT sessions I've been doing helped me recognize I was avoiding things, but logging in still felt like a chore.
Q4: For a few weeks in the middle of the semester, I got into a habit of checking the LMS in the morning. I think it was after one of my CBT sessions where we talked about small habits. It didn't last though. Once I missed a few days, the routine just fell apart and I went back to only logging in when I absolutely had to.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were most useful for me since that's how I learn best. I could download PDFs and go through them at my own pace. Discussion posts were okay too because I could see what other people were thinking without having to talk out loud. Videos were fine but I didn't always have the energy to sit through them.
Q6: I pretty much ignored optional readings and supplementary videos. If it wasn't going to be graded, I couldn't motivate myself to care. Some of the interactive activities felt pointless too, like busy work. When you're already struggling to do the minimum, extra stuff just feels overwhelming.
Q7: If it was directly connected to an assignment or exam, it felt worth my time. I needed to see a clear payoff. Anything that seemed vague or optional, I'd skip. My motivation is pretty low, so I have to be strategic about where I put my limited energy. If I couldn't see how it helped my grade, it got ignored.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt completely different. Required activities had this weight to them, like I had to do them or face consequences. Optional things just disappeared into the background. I know I probably missed out on useful stuff, but when you're barely keeping your head above water, optional feels like a luxury I couldn't afford.
Q9: All the time. I'd download the readings and study from the PDFs on my laptop without being connected to the LMS. I also used my own notes and sometimes looked things up on external sites. A lot of my actual studying happened offline, just me and my highlighted documents. The LMS wouldn't have seen most of that.
Q10: There were nights where I'd spend hours going through downloaded materials, making notes by hand, trying to understand concepts. But the LMS would just show that I logged in once to download something. All that time wrestling with the content in my room, feeling sad but still pushing through, none of that shows up anywhere.
Q11: Yeah, definitely. I'd download lecture slides or readings once, then never go back to the LMS for them. I'd study from those files for weeks. It was easier to have everything saved locally than to keep logging in. The LMS probably thought I only looked at things briefly when really I was reviewing them multiple times offline.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard. I was feeling pretty sad and disconnected from everything. My attendance dropped and I just couldn't make myself show up. The stress was building but I felt paralyzed. It wasn't that I didn't know what to do, I just couldn't make myself do it. Everything felt pointless.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was tired or stressed, the LMS was the last place I wanted to be. Competing responsibilities from my extracurriculars and just life stuff made it worse. Sometimes I'd be so mentally exhausted that even logging in felt like climbing a mountain. Confusion about what was actually required also made me avoid things longer than I should have.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. My eyes would move across the screen but my brain was somewhere else, usually stuck in that sad fog. I'd watch videos but realize at the end I couldn't remember what they said. It was like going through the motions without actually being there.
Q15: All the time. I'd know an assignment was important but still put it off. Part of it was the sadness making everything feel pointless. Part of it was just low motivation, like I couldn't connect future consequences to present actions. The CBT sessions helped me understand this pattern, but knowing about it didn't always stop it from happening.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire system. Without them, I probably wouldn't have done anything. I'd wait until the pressure was unbearable, then finally act. It's not healthy, I know. But that panic was the only thing that could cut through the fog and make me actually complete things. My assignment completion rate is high because deadlines forced me.
Q17: Feedback sometimes helped, sometimes made things worse. Good feedback gave me a small boost, but critical feedback just confirmed what I already felt, that I wasn't good enough. I'd sometimes avoid checking grades because I didn't want to feel worse. When I did get positive comments, it was nice but the feeling didn't last long.
Q18: The structure was okay, but it didn't really push me to engage more than necessary. Weekly modules gave me some framework, but I'd still procrastinate until the last minute. If things were more broken down into smaller chunks with more frequent check-ins, maybe I would have stayed on track better. The open-endedness gave me too much room to drift.
Q19: The discussion requirements actually helped because they forced me to interact with peers, which I wouldn't have done otherwise. But some of the longer assignments felt overwhelming when I was already struggling. Having clearer expectations and maybe more structure would have made engagement easier. The flexibility that was supposed to help actually let me fall behind.
Q20: I think that's me sometimes. I'd be clicking around, watching videos, reading discussions, but not actually producing anything. It's like treading water without swimming anywhere. You're active but not accomplishing the things that matter. The engagement is there but it's unfocused, maybe even a form of procrastination.
Q21: That makes sense to me. I did a lot of studying offline that wouldn't show up. Some people just learn efficiently without needing to be constantly logged in. Or they have good notes from class and don't need the LMS as much. Low activity doesn't mean low effort, it might just mean different methods.
Q22: Logs can show when you logged in, what you clicked, how long you stayed. But they can't show if you were actually thinking or just staring at the screen. They can't capture offline studying, emotional state, or whether the learning actually stuck. They show behavior, not understanding or real engagement.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning, being mentally present, wanting to understand not just pass. For me, I was rarely truly engaged this semester. I was going through motions, doing enough to get by. Real engagement would mean I'd think about the material outside of deadlines, which I almost never did.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I was fighting just to do the minimum. It wouldn't show the sadness that made everything feel heavy, or the CBT sessions that helped me get through. It wouldn't show the offline studying, the internal struggles, or how much effort it took just to submit assignments. The data sees activity, not the human behind it.
